findings.title: roic-trend.js uses IC = assets-cash-debt; roic.js uses IC = assets-cash only
findings.description: roic-trend.js computes investedCapital as totalAssets - totalCash - totalDebt (line 60). roic.js uses totalAssets - totalCash ONLY (line 22). The two methods that are supposed to measure the same concept (ROIC level vs ROIC trend) use different denominators. For any company with debt, roic-trend reports higher absolute ROIC values than roic.js, making the 'trend' delta incommensurable with the 'level' threshold. The sector-medians-compute.js ROIC bucket also uses the assets-cash formula (matching roic.js), so the auto-median threshold calibrated for roic.js will be wrong when applied via effectiveThreshold to any future roic-trend usage.
findings.mechanism: roic.js IC = assets - cash. roic-trend.js IC = assets - cash - debt. For MSFT-scale ($97B debt): IC difference = 22%. roic-trend ROIC is 22% higher in absolute terms. The trend delta (roic[0]-roic[1]) is internally consistent but the absolute levels are not comparable to the roic.js pass threshold.
findings.reproducer: Any leveraged stock: MSFT totalDebt ~$97B, totalCash ~$80B, totalAssets ~$512B. roic.js IC=432B; roic-trend.js IC=335B. Same NI produces roic 29% higher in roic-trend.js.
findings.impact: Score-aggregator comparisons between roic (level) and roic-trend (delta) are based on incommensurable denominators. CORE method mismatch can mislead trend-direction interpretation.
findings.suggested_fix: Align roic-trend.js line 60 to: `const invested = ta - tc;` (drop `- td`). Or align roic.js to also subtract debt — pick one formula and apply consistently to both methods and sector-medians-compute.

findings.title: sector-medians-compute.js only computes 3 metrics — all other methods cannot get auto-calibrated thresholds
findings.description: gatherBySubProfile initialises buckets with exactly 3 keys: `{ roic: [], roce: [], 'fcf-yield': [] }`. All other method IDs (gross-margin-stability, revenue-growth-3y, net-debt-ebitda, earnings-stability, etc.) will never find an entry in the auto-median files. effectiveThreshold falls through to static sector-medians.json or the method default for all non-covered methods. The Tag 167 regional calibration feature therefore only benefits 3 metrics, while the header comment implies broader coverage.
findings.mechanism: Only 3 metric IDs are ever pushed into globalBuckets/regionalBuckets. Any other method ID produces no auto-median and always uses static or default threshold.
findings.reproducer: effectiveThreshold(stock, 'gross-margin-stability', 0.35) — auto-medians has no 'gross-margin-stability' key. Returns { threshold: 0.35, source: 'default' } for all stocks worldwide, including Japanese industrials where 35% GM is a premium not a floor.
findings.impact: All methods except roic, roce, and fcf-yield use static or default thresholds regardless of sector or region. Regional calibration is effectively non-functional for the majority of the method registry.
findings.suggested_fix: Expand gatherBySubProfile to also compute buckets for 'gross-margin-stability', 'revenue-growth-3y', 'fcf-stability', 'earnings-stability'. Gross profit and operating income are already extracted in the same loop — the marginal cost is low.

findings.title: rule-of-40.js: TTM FCF fallback can fire on structurally-declining FCF stocks
findings.description: The Tag 201c fallback (lines 54-60) substitutes a 3-year annual FCF margin median when TTM is negative AND the annual median exceeds 5%. The guard condition only checks `annualMedian > 5` — there is no check that the MOST RECENT annual FCF is also positive. A stock with annual FCF margins of [+8%, +6%, -50%] has a 3-year median of +6% (above threshold), so Rule-of-40 substitutes +6% for a genuinely negative TTM, inflating the R40 score by up to 18+ pp. The fallback was designed for MELI (transient WC dip with structurally positive FCF) but fires on stocks with declining FCF where the most recent year is the worst.
findings.mechanism: `_annualFcfMarginMedian` returns median of up to 4 annual points requiring only 3 to be valid. A stock with 2 strong historical years and 1 deeply negative recent year passes the `annualMedian > 5` check, triggering the substitution even though the underlying FCF is deteriorating.
findings.reproducer: annualFCF = [strong, strong, deeply negative]; fcfMarginTTM = -12%. 3-year median > 5% → fallback fires. R40 score uses historical median instead of current negative FCF.
findings.impact: CORE method — false passes bubble to top of all mode rankings. Stocks with deteriorating FCF can pass Rule-of-40 when they should fail. This is the primary hypergrowth signal.
findings.suggested_fix: Add guard: only apply fallback when most recent annual FCF is also positive. `const fcfArr = stock && stock.annual && stock.annual.annualFCF || []; if (fcfMargin < 0 && _unwrap(fcfArr[0]) > 0) { const annualMedian = ...; }`

findings.title: sector-medians-compute.js: POSITIVE_ONLY_METRICS fallback defeats itself on distressed sectors
findings.description: Line 152: `const thresholdArr = POSITIVE_ONLY_METRICS.has(mid) ? arr.filter(v => v > -0.05) : arr;`. If thresholdArr.length < minN (too few positive-ROIC stocks), line 155 falls back to the full arr: `const useArr = thresholdArr.length >= minN ? thresholdArr : arr;`. The minimum-sample guard was passed on arr.length (line 150), not thresholdArr.length. So in a distressed sector with 5 stocks but only 2 positive ROIC: thresholdArr.length=2, minN=5 → falls back to full arr. The sector median now includes deeply negative ROIC values, producing a negative threshold that makes most stocks look above-median regardless of their actual ROIC.
findings.mechanism: The minN check is on the raw array but the fallback to full arr occurs when the filtered array is too small. In distressed sectors the filter is exactly where it's needed, but the fallback defeats it.
findings.reproducer: 5-stock sector with ROIC = [-40%, -20%, -10%, +8%, +15%]. thresholdArr = [+8%, +15%] (length 2 < minN=5). useArr = full arr. Median = -10%. Any stock with ROIC > -10% looks 'above sector median'.
findings.impact: Sector ROIC thresholds are inverted for distressed sectors. The effectiveThreshold system gives a negative ROIC threshold to non-distressed stocks in a sector with many loss-makers, causing them to look unrealistically good.
findings.suggested_fix: When POSITIVE_ONLY_METRICS and thresholdArr.length < minN, skip writing the median entirely (do not fall back to full arr). Replace line 155 with: `if (POSITIVE_ONLY_METRICS.has(mid) && thresholdArr.length < minN) continue; const useArr = thresholdArr.length >= minN ? thresholdArr : arr;`

findings.title: deceleration-guard.js: missing TTM growth silently bypasses the DATAGUARD (always pass)
findings.description: Line 42: `var hardFail = ttmGrowth != null && ttmGrowth >= 25 && qYoY < 10 && qBelowAvg;`. The `ttmGrowth != null` precondition means if `metrics.revenueGrowthYoY` is missing, hardFail is always false and pass=true. Line 44 soft-fail also gates on `ttmGrowth != null`. So any stock with missing TTM growth metric returns computable:true, pass:true — the guard is fully bypassed with no signal that it had no data to evaluate. This is a DATAGUARD — silent bypass is a hard miss.
findings.mechanism: Both hardFail and softFail conditions require ttmGrowth != null. When absent, both are false, pass=!false=true, result is computable:true pass:true. No incomputable flag is returned.
findings.reproducer: Stock missing metrics.revenueGrowthYoY but with qRev[0] << qRev[4] (accelerating decline). Guard returns pass:true, computable:true. Stock appears clean to all downstream consumers.
findings.impact: Stocks with genuinely decelerating revenue but missing TTM growth metric bypass this DATAGUARD. They appear in hypergrowth mode rankings without the deceleration penalty.
findings.suggested_fix: When ttmGrowth is null, return computable:false: add before line 42: `if (ttmGrowth == null) return H.buildResult({ computable: false, reason: 'ttmGrowth null — cannot assess deceleration', threshold: 10, thresholdOp: 'gte' });`

findings.title: generate-modes-report.js: fcfMargin sentinel -999 hides cards when FCF slider is moved
findings.description: Lines 345-346: when rule-of-40 is not computable, `fcfMargin` is set to -999. Line 386 embeds this as `data-fcf-margin="-999.0"`. The JS filter on line 1110: `var fcfOk = (fcfMin <= -30) || (Number.isFinite(fcfM) && fcfM > -100 && fcfM >= fcfMin);` blocks -999 (< -100) whenever fcfMin > -30 (i.e., any time the user has moved the FCF slider at all). Cards for stocks where Rule-of-40 is not computable are hidden whenever the user applies any FCF filter, even though those stocks may be excellent Quality-Compounder or Buffett candidates.
findings.mechanism: Sentinel -999 < -100 → fcfM > -100 is false → fcfOk = false → card hidden. User moves slider from default, valid stocks disappear with no explanation.
findings.reproducer: Load modes-report.html. Adjust FCF Min slider above -30%. All stocks where r40.computable=false vanish from all mode views.
findings.impact: Valid non-computable-R40 stocks (common in Quality-Compounder and Buffett modes) silently disappear from the UI when the FCF filter is touched. Users see a reduced universe without knowing why.
findings.suggested_fix: Change sentinel from -999 to a distinguishable string ('n/a') in the data attribute. Update JS filter: `var fcfOk = (fcfMin <= -30) || fcfM === 'n/a' || (Number.isFinite(fcfM) && fcfM >= fcfMin);` — treat incomputable FCF as filter-neutral.

findings.title: generate-modes-report.js: revGrowth sentinel -999 also hides cards when growth slider is moved
findings.description: Same pattern as F-ME-006. Line 346: when r40 is not computable, `revGrowth = -999`. Line 386 embeds as `data-rev-growth="-999.0"`. JS filter line 1111: `var growthOk = (growthMin <= 0) || (Number.isFinite(revG) && revG > -100 && revG >= growthMin);` blocks -999 whenever growthMin > 0. Moving the Growth slider above 0% hides all non-computable-R40 stocks, regardless of their actual revenue growth signal.
findings.mechanism: -999 < -100 → revG > -100 is false → growthOk = false → card hidden whenever slider > 0%.
findings.reproducer: Move Growth Min slider to any value > 0%. All stocks where r40.computable=false disappear from view.
findings.impact: Same as F-ME-006 — stocks filtered from UI silently when growth slider touched.
findings.suggested_fix: Same approach as F-ME-006: use 'n/a' sentinel and treat it as filter-neutral in the JS filter.

findings.title: revenue-volatility-guard.js: ttmRev fallback uses || instead of ?? — zero TTM revenue bypasses material floor
findings.description: Line 73: `const ttmRev = H.metricValue(stock, 'revenueTTM') || (Number.isFinite(revY[0]) ? revY[0] : 0);`. The `||` operator treats 0 as falsy. If `H.metricValue` returns exactly 0 (explicitly zero revenue TTM), the fallback fires and revY[0] is used instead. A pre-revenue company with `metrics.revenueTTM = {value: 0}` would use revY[0] — which might be positive from a prior period — bypassing the $100M material floor check. The same issue appears at the end: `0 || revY[0]` substitutes the most-recent annual value even though the TTM was explicitly zero.
findings.mechanism: `0 || fallback` evaluates the fallback when the primary is 0. H.metricValue correctly returns 0 for zero-valued metrics, but `||` treats that as 'missing'.
findings.reproducer: metrics.revenueTTM = {value: 0}. H.metricValue returns 0. `0 || revY[0]` → uses revY[0]. If revY[0] = 200M, toUsdRough = 200M >= 100M, material floor passed. Full volatility analysis runs on what is actually a zero-TTM-revenue stock.
findings.impact: Pre-revenue or winding-down companies with explicitly zero TTM revenue may incorrectly pass the material floor check. Low practical impact (most will also have near-zero historical revenue), but it is a latent correctness issue.
findings.suggested_fix: Use nullish coalescing: `const ttmRev = H.metricValue(stock, 'revenueTTM') ?? (Number.isFinite(revY[0]) ? revY[0] : 0);`

findings.title: sector-medians-compute.js: rolling median does not prune stale metrics within surviving sub-profiles
findings.description: Lines 322-327 prune entire sub-profiles from the rolling cache when they drop below minN: `const currentSpIds = new Set(Object.keys(autoMedians)); for (const spId...) { if (!currentSpIds.has(spId)) delete merged[spId]; }`. However, individual metrics within a surviving sub-profile that are absent from the current run (e.g., 'fcf-yield' dropped below minN this run) are NOT pruned. The stale metric remains in merged[spId] and accumulates without getting new values. effectiveThreshold will find a rolling12mMedian with >= 12 weekly samples for this metric and use it — a stale calibration.
findings.mechanism: Pruning operates at sub-profile level only. Metric-level pruning does not exist. A metric absent from autoMedians[spId] in the current run stays in merged[spId] from all prior runs.
findings.reproducer: SAAS sub-profile has 'fcf-yield' in rolling cache from 12 prior weeks. Current week: SAAS has < 5 stocks with FCF data → 'fcf-yield' absent from autoMedians['SAAS']. Rolling cache retains the 12-week stale entry. effectiveThreshold selects it via rolling12m path.
findings.impact: Stale sector-relative FCF yield thresholds persist after underlying data becomes sparse. Moderate impact on ROIC/FCF threshold calibration for affected sectors.
findings.suggested_fix: After the sub-profile pruning loop, add metric-level pruning: `for (const spId of Object.keys(merged)) { if (autoMedians[spId]) { for (const mid of Object.keys(merged[spId])) { if (!autoMedians[spId][mid] && !mid.startsWith('rolling') && !mid.startsWith('asOf')) delete merged[spId][mid]; } } }`

findings.title: _helpers.js buildResult: string values ('VOLATILE', 'CONSISTENT', etc.) are silently converted to null
findings.description: buildResult line 60: `value: value != null && Number.isFinite(value) ? value : null`. This converts ANY non-numeric value to null. revenue-volatility-guard.js returns `value: 'VOLATILE'`, `value: 'SPLIT_ARTIFACT'`, `value: 'CONSISTENT'`, `value: 'IMMATERIAL'`. All four string values are silently dropped — buildResult stores null for the value field. The reason string contains the classification text, but machine-readable result.value is always null for these methods.
findings.mechanism: `Number.isFinite('VOLATILE')` = false → value stored as null. Any downstream code that checks `result.value === 'VOLATILE'` will never match. The string semantics are present only in the reason field.
findings.reproducer: Call revenue-volatility-guard.evaluate() on a volatile stock. result.value = null (not 'VOLATILE').
findings.impact: Any code path that routes on result.value for categorical methods will see null. Currently the string values are not machine-tested downstream, so immediate impact is low — but it is a trap for future code and makes the API inconsistent.
findings.suggested_fix: Allow string values in buildResult: `value: (value != null && (Number.isFinite(value) || typeof value === 'string')) ? value : null`. Alternatively, use a dedicated `components.classification` field for string-typed results and update all methods consistently.

findings.title: profitability-trend.js: both-exactly-zero NI classified as FLAT (pass) — zero-NI companies pass CORE method
findings.description: Lines 62-63: `// Both zero - edge case\n  return 'FLAT';`. When y0 = 0 AND y1 = 0, the function returns FLAT. FLAT maps to trendRank=1 and pass=true. A company with exactly zero net income for two consecutive years passes profitability-trend. While edge-case in theory, it can occur for SPACs, pre-revenue shells, or companies with precisely offsetting one-time items. A zero-NI company arguably should return incomputable, not pass.
findings.mechanism: The both-zero branch bypasses sign checks and returns FLAT. FLAT is pass=true. Zero-NI companies pass this CORE method.
findings.impact: Zero-NI companies can pass profitability-trend CORE method. Most will fail other guards (profitability-state=LOSS), limiting real exposure, but the CORE logic is permissive when it shouldn't be.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the both-zero return to incomputable: `if (y0 === 0 && y1 === 0) return null;` (the null propagates to the incomputable branch in evaluate()).

findings.title: profitability-trend.js: sign-flip IMPROVING fires on $1 NI improvement from -$10B loss
findings.description: Line 44: `if (y0 > 0 && y1 <= 0) return 'IMPROVING';`. Any strictly positive current NI combined with any non-positive prior NI triggers IMPROVING regardless of magnitude. A company that went from -$10B to +$1 NI (one-time gain restoring technical profitability) is classified IMPROVING with the same confidence as a genuine turnaround. There is no materiality gate — a $1 difference from a $10B loss scenario triggers IMPROVING just as much as a genuine recovery.
findings.mechanism: Binary sign check: `y0 > 0` is strictly positive for any positive value including near-zero. Combined with `y1 <= 0` (prior year non-positive), any nominal profit triggers IMPROVING.
findings.reproducer: y0 = 1 (one dollar NI, one-time gain), y1 = -5000000000 (five billion loss). Both conditions true → IMPROVING, pass=true.
findings.impact: Companies with trivially positive NI due to one-time gains after large losses are classified IMPROVING by a CORE method. This feeds into mode scoring for TURNAROUND candidates.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a materiality gate requiring the sign-flip to be at least 1% of revenue: extract revenue from the stock object and require `Math.abs(y0) > Math.abs(latestRev) * 0.01` before returning IMPROVING.

findings.title: generate-modes-report.js: topAllMust still uses O(M) Runner.METHODS.find instead of pre-built METHODS_BY_ID map
findings.description: Line 260: `const sortMethodMeta = sortMethodId ? Runner.METHODS.find(m => m.id === sortMethodId) : null;` inside `topAllMust()`. METHODS_BY_ID map was built at module scope (line 28) specifically to replace O(M) linear scans. The chips rendering loop (line 369) and near-misses panel (line 469) were already migrated to METHODS_BY_ID.get(). topAllMust() was overlooked, remaining as an O(M) scan called once per mode (4 times per report generation).
findings.mechanism: topAllMust() calls Runner.METHODS.find() per invocation. With ~83 methods and 4 modes: 4 × 83 = ~332 comparisons per report. Not performance-critical but inconsistent with the stated fix audit F-222a-8.
findings.reproducer: Any call to topAllMust() with a non-null sortMethodId.
findings.impact: Performance inconsistency only. No correctness risk. Minor oversight in the stated METHODS_BY_ID migration.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace line 260: `const sortMethodMeta = sortMethodId ? METHODS_BY_ID.get(sortMethodId) : null;`

findings.title: estimate-revision-proxy.js: single null base quarter aborts all 4 growth computations
findings.description: Lines 37-40 in _revenueAcceleration: `if (curr == null || base == null || base <= 0) return null;`. If ANY of the 4 base-period quarters (rq[4..7]) has a null or zero value, the function returns null immediately, abandoning all 4 growth computations. A pre-commercialization company that had zero revenue in one prior quarter but positive revenue in others loses the entire revenue-acceleration signal. This hits exactly the pre-breakout stage companies (recent commercial revenue) that the method is designed to find.
findings.mechanism: The loop on lines 36-41 returns null on first failure. Single null/zero base quarter kills all 4 iterations.
findings.reproducer: rq[7] = 0 (Q7 had zero revenue). Loop i=3: base = rq[7] = 0. `base <= 0` → return null. All 4 growths abandoned. Method returns computable:false.
findings.impact: Companies that had zero revenue in a single quarter 7 quarters ago lose the acceleration signal entirely. These are likely the most interesting early-stage compounder candidates.
findings.suggested_fix: Collect valid growth values and skip invalid quarters: `const growths = []; for (let i=0; i<4; i++) { const curr = _val(rq[i]); const base = _val(rq[i+4]); if (curr == null || base == null || base <= 0) continue; growths.push((curr-base)/Math.abs(base)); } if (growths.length < 2) return null;`

findings.title: generate-modes-report.js: loadStocks parse failures not tracked in _pipelineFailures
findings.description: Lines 95-100: async batch reader wraps JSON.parse in try/catch and returns null on error. Parse failures are silently dropped. _pipelineFailures (tracked in evaluateAll, line 127-130) only captures evaluation failures — NOT load failures. If 10% of snapshot files have truncated JSON (e.g., from an interrupted write), those stocks are silently absent from the universe. The pipeline health report shows 0% failure rate even though a significant fraction of the universe was lost at load time.
findings.mechanism: loadStocks parse errors → null → filtered out → missing from stocks array. stocks.length reduced. _pipelineFailures[] empty. Health report: n_failed=0, failure_rate=0%.
findings.reproducer: Inject 100 truncated snapshot files. loadStocks silently drops them. stocks.length reduced by 100. Health report shows clean pipeline.
findings.impact: Silent data loss from parse failures goes unreported. Rankings are based on a reduced universe without any health alert. The 5% failure threshold is ineffective for this failure mode.
findings.suggested_fix: Track parse failures in loadStocks and merge them into the health report: `const _loadFailures = []; ... catch (e) { _loadFailures.push({ file: f, error: e.message }); return null; }`. Add load failures to the healthReport JSON object.

findings.title: single-quarter-dependency.js: priorScaled 0.75 factor is not seasonal-safe
findings.description: Line 109: `const priorScaled = priorTtm * 0.75;`. After removing maxQ (largest of recent 4), ttmExMax represents a 3-quarter window. This is compared to priorTtm * 0.75 (3/4 of prior TTM) as an apples-to-apples basis. However, the dropped quarter is the LARGEST recent quarter, which for seasonal businesses is typically the same calendar quarter each year (e.g., Q4 for retailers). priorScaled = priorTtm * 0.75 assumes the dropped quarter is a uniform 25% of annual revenue. For a seasonal business where Q4 = 40% of revenue, priorScaled overstates the comparison base, making collapse appear larger than it is.
findings.mechanism: Uniform 0.75 scaling assumes equal quarterly revenue distribution. For seasonal businesses, the largest quarter is systematically larger than 25% — the comparison is not apples-to-apples.
findings.reproducer: Seasonal business: quarterly split [20%, 20%, 20%, 40%]. maxQ = Q4 (40% of TTM). ttmExMax = 60% of TTM. priorScaled = 75% of prior TTM. Collapse metric inflated for seasonal businesses.
findings.impact: DIAGNOSTIC method — score drag only. Seasonal businesses (retail, enterprise software) may incorrectly fail.
findings.suggested_fix: Identify which position in recent[0..3] is maxQ, then exclude the matching position in prior[0..3] from the comparison window, rather than using a uniform 0.75 scale.

findings.title: generate-modes-report.js: sectorExcluded metric hardcodes HYPERGROWTH mode
findings.description: Line 553: `const sectorExcluded = totalStocks - eligibleByMode.HYPERGROWTH.length;`. The status strip 'Sektor-Exclude' displays the difference between total stocks and HYPERGROWTH-eligible stocks. Each mode applies different sector exclusion rules. If BUFFETT or TURNAROUND exclude more sectors than HYPERGROWTH, the displayed exclusion count is understated for those modes.
findings.mechanism: eligibleByMode is computed for all modes. Only HYPERGROWTH.length is used for the display metric.
findings.reproducer: BUFFETT mode excludes 300 stocks, HYPERGROWTH excludes 200. Status strip always shows 200 regardless of which tab is active.
findings.impact: Minor UI inaccuracy — informational strip only. Low impact.
findings.suggested_fix: Show min(eligible lengths) across all modes (most restrictive), or make the count mode-aware in JS when tabs switch, or label it explicitly as 'HYPERGROWTH exclusion'.

files_skipped: methods/gross-margin-stability.js (not returned by glob — may exist under different name)
methods/score-aggregator.js (not in glob results — outside explicit scope)
Various other REGISTRY-listed method files not returned by glob
